title: Is Jesus Enough? Finding Peace in Adversity
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: Is Jesus Enough_ Finding Peace in Adversity - Elder Scott Hollander - Psalm 13.txt

outline.title: Even Christians Face Deep Despair
subpoints: David — a man after God's own heart, filled with the Spirit, patriarch of the Messianic line — fell into deep depression and wrote Psalm 13
The fourfold cry 'how long' (vv. 1-2) is asked twenty-two times throughout the Psalms; David is exhausted, at his wit's end, unsure how much more he can bear
David feels alone, abandoned by God — like the person in the footprints poem; yet God had not hidden himself but was present all along
The Great Smog of London (1952): Big Ben, the Tower of London, and Lady Justice were invisible — but they were still there; God's presence is like that in our darkest moments
Psalm 139:7-12: there is no place where God is not — even in darkness, the night is bright as the day to him

outline.title: David Allows His Emotions to Drive His Thinking
subpoints: David says he 'takes counsel in his soul' — he is seeking advice from his emotions rather than from what he knows in his mind about God
He allows his feelings and perceptions to dictate his understanding of reality, which deepens the despair
Elder Hollander shares a personal example from deployment in Afghanistan: feeling low, emailing his wife, and receiving the reply 'drink water' — a perspective-changer in the morning
Jeremiah's lament in Lamentations 3: the steadfast love of the Lord never ceases; his mercies are new every morning — a morning-light perspective

outline.title: The Means of Grace Tether the Weary to Christ
subpoints: The means of grace — prayer, the preaching of the Word, and the sacraments — are the ordinary means in which every believer, especially the weary, is tethered to Christ
Like a balloon on a string or a tarp with a single anchor strap, you may feel you are flapping in your misery, but the means of grace secure you to Christ
David's supplication in verse 3 — 'consider and hear me, O Lord my God' — moves from weeping to asking: give light to my eyes
Psalm 18: in his distress David called upon the Lord and cried out; God heard his voice from his temple
Dr. Kurtow of Greenville Presbyterian: if you want to get hit by a train, you play on the railroad tracks; if you want to find grace, you go to where the means of grace can be found

outline.title: Peace Returns in the Light of Jesus
subpoints: David closes with trust in God's hesed — the covenant faithfulness, the steadfast love of God promised to his covenant children
The three Hebrew men before the fiery furnace: our God is able to deliver us; but even if he does not, we do not serve your gods — and Christ met them in the furnace
Job 19:25-27: I know that my Redeemer lives, and in my flesh I shall see God — facing physical death with confidence in the resurrection
John 20: Mary in the garden, weeping, not recognizing Jesus — until he simply said her name, 'Mary'; in that one word, perspective changed completely
Psalm 30:5: weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning — the Lord is risen and he is with us

summary:
Elder Scott Hollander preaches from Psalm 13 — one of the great lament Psalms — in what appears to be a season of pandemic isolation. The sermon's driving question is: 'Is Jesus enough?' and its thesis is that a Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair. Three points follow David's progression through the six verses of the Psalm: even Christians face deep despair; the means of grace tether the weary to Christ; and peace returns in the light of Jesus.

The sermon opens by establishing that David — a man after God's own heart, filled with the Holy Spirit, the Spirit-anointed patriarch of the Messianic lineage — fell into deep depression. His fourfold 'how long' in verses 1-2 echoes twenty-two times throughout the Psalter. David feels abandoned and alone, but Elder Hollander uses the Great Smog of London (1952) as an extended illustration: when the city was blanketed in deadly fog so dense residents could not see their own feet, Big Ben and the Tower of London were invisible — but they were there. God's hiddenness in dark seasons is not God's absence; Psalm 139 confirms there is no place where he is not.

The means of grace receive extended treatment as the practical pathway to peace: prayer, the preaching of the Word, and the sacraments are the ordinary means in which the weary are anchored to Christ. David models this by moving from moaning (vv. 1-2) to supplication (vv. 3-4) to praise (vv. 5-6). The sermon closes with the Resurrection appearance to Mary in John 20: in the depths of her grief, unable to see through her tears, Jesus simply said her name — and in that one word, everything changed. The Lord is risen, he is present, and peace returns in the light of Jesus.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 13

If you are able, would you please remain standing? Turn in your Bibles with me to Psalm 13. We'll read Psalm 13 together.

*To the Chief Musician. A Psalm of David. How long, O Lord? Will You forget me forever? How long will You hide Your face from me? How long shall I take counsel in my soul, having sorrow in my heart daily? How long will my enemy be exalted over me? Consider and hear me, O Lord my God; and lighten my eyes, lest I sleep the sleep of death; lest my enemy say, "I have prevailed against him"; lest those who trouble me rejoice when I am moved. But I have trusted in Your mercy; my heart shall rejoice in Your salvation. I will sing to the Lord, because He has dealt bountifully with me.*

Lord, we do pray Your blessing on the reading of this word and the preaching of it. In Christ's name we pray. Amen. You may be seated.

## Introduction: The Question Before Us

I hope that you all can agree with me when I say that God is good, and God is good all the time. God is good when things seem good for us, when things are going well. And God is good when things are seemingly bad for us.

Two weeks ago in our responsive reading, I was struck with the straightforwardness of this particular verse — Psalm 119:71: *It is good for me that I have been afflicted, that I may learn Your statutes.* And David goes on to state: *I know, O Lord, that Your judgments are right, and that in faithfulness You have afflicted me.* God is faithful in his dispensing of afflictions. He especially uses our afflictions to train us in righteousness. Do not dread them.

It has been said too often lately that we are living in unprecedented times. The times are certainly unique to us, but it seems every generation faces their own form of a global or communal crisis. Even last century, consider the hardships faced by world wars, the Great Depression, and the Spanish flu. The Bubonic Plague preceded that in the fourteenth and nineteenth centuries.

Regardless, these times and the restrictions that people faced during quarantine, mask-wearing, and social distancing have been shown to bear much ill effect. We have even lost the ability for that simple pleasant smile to greet another customer in the grocery store, with our faces half obscured. And if you're paranoid like me, you have to consciously remind yourself that these people probably don't want to kill you as their eyes attempt to pierce your soul.

Benedict Carey writes in the New York Times: "The ultimate marker of the virus's mental toll, some experts say, will show up in the nation's suicide rate in this and coming years." In the same article, he quotes Matthew Nock, a psychology professor at Harvard: "There's not only an increase in anxiety, but the more important piece is social isolation. We've never had anything like this, and we know social isolation is related to suicide."

A thirty-five-year-old college instructor said: "I see all the bad, the suffering, and a tendency to crawl into a hole. Now with this COVID threat we're being told to isolate and stay away from others. It's like — oh, I was right all along, and the world was crazy."

Regardless of pandemics and the isolation many are facing now, life has always had its ebbs and flows of difficulties. Many have lost jobs, faced divorce, lost family members to death, experienced relationship problems within the home and even the workplace, dealt with chronic illness or pain. The effects of sin ravage the human body and soul. Sometimes it seems like there is no reprieve. We feel emotionally drained, exhausted, overcome with grief. We face depression, suicidal thoughts, and all kinds of thoughts and emotions are beating us down.

Whether in especially trying times or the normal struggles and hardships of life, the perspective of Christians needs to be evidently different. My goal today is not to discuss the merits of what types or manifestations of grief and depression are acceptable and what types are sinful. What I want to do this morning is answer the question: is Jesus enough?

When you or your friend are in the depths of despair, I want you to ask yourself: is Jesus enough? And I want you to answer — and in fact Scripture compels us to answer this way: yes. I can find peace even in my depression. Friend, you can find peace even in your adversity. Scripture declares for the Christian: a Christian can find peace even in deep despair.

By examining our text from Psalm 13, I want us to become convinced that a Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair. We will follow David in his struggle and follow his threefold progression: his cries of despair, his request for comfort, and then his praise — demonstrating his peace in Christ. As we follow David, we will confirm three main points:

1. Even Christians face deep despair.
2. The means of grace tether the weary to Christ.
3. Peace returns in the light of Jesus.

## Point 1: Even Christians Face Deep Despair

The first thing we need to do is acknowledge that even Christians face deep despair.

The opening of the Psalm states that its author is none other than David. Both the Old Testament (1 Samuel 13-14) and the New Testament (Acts 13:22) tell us that David was a man after God's own heart. At his anointing, 1 Samuel 16:13 tells us the Spirit of the Lord came upon David from that day forward. David was the patriarch of the Messianic lineage leading up to the birth of Jesus. And through further inspiration, David is presumed to have written seventy-five of our hundred and fifty Psalms. David was a Holy Spirit-filled man of God.

Noticeably in his cry "how long, O Lord," he is making a personal plea to Yahweh — the covenantal name of God. Yahweh was David's God, whom he loved, worshiped, and served. Believers — even David, stamped with the approval of God himself as a man after his own heart — fell into deep depression. Yet a Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair, as David demonstrates for us.

Look again at verses 1 and 2:

*How long, O Lord? Will You forget me forever? How long will You hide Your face from me? How long shall I take counsel in my soul, having sorrow in my heart daily? How long will my enemy be exalted over me?*

In these two verses the phrase "how long" is asked by David four times. One pastor counts twenty-two times that this question is raised throughout the Psalms. David is tired, exhausted, at his wit's end. His struggle had been long, and he is unsure how much longer he can bear it — how much more he can take — whether he can endure. *How long must I hold out? Will you forget me forever?*

David is in utter despair — a feeling of being alone and helpless before God, and he can't see God, and he wonders if God has forgotten him. I'm sure you recall the man from the footprints poem, saying: "God, I'm looking back and in my hours of adversity I'm only seeing one set of footprints. Why have you abandoned me?" But he was wrong, wasn't he? The author of the poem asserts that he was not only not abandoned, but that he had been carried through his adversity.

Are any of you familiar with the Great Smog of London? Well, at that time London was blanketed with deep, dark, and deadly fog. Historian Christopher Klein writes: "For five days in December 1952, the Great Smog of London smothered the city, wreaking havoc and killing thousands." He goes on: "The smog was so dense that residents in some sections of the city were unable to see their feet as they walked. Public and private transportation came to a halt, some even abandoning their vehicles where they stopped. Criminals were emboldened in their ability to vanish into the darkness." In the darkness, the Tower of London, Big Ben, and Lady Justice were invisible to the eye — but they were there nonetheless.

In the garden, Adam and Eve hid from God because of the guilt of their sin. Yet God had not hidden himself from them. Just because a believer may be in the depths of despair and can't see God, God has not abandoned him, God has not abandoned her.

Remember the cry of David from Psalm 139: *Where shall I go from Your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from Your presence? If I ascend into heaven, You are there; if I make my bed in hell, behold, You are there. If I take the wings of the morning, and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea, even there Your hand shall lead me, and Your right hand shall hold me. If I say, "Surely the darkness shall fall on me," even the night shall be light about me; indeed, the darkness shall not hide from You, but the night shines as the day; the darkness and the light are both alike to You.*

Even when you think all hope is lost and you feel alone, remember: there is no place where God is not.

The Apostle Paul declares this so richly. Romans 8, starting at verse 31:

*What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare His own Son, but delivered Him up for us all, how shall He not with Him also freely give us all things? Who shall bring a charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is he who condemns? It is Christ who died, and furthermore is also risen, who is even at the right hand of God, who also makes intercession for us. Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword? As it is written: "For Your sake we are killed all day long; we are accounted as sheep for the slaughter." Yet in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor principalities nor powers, nor things present nor things to come, nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.*

In David's struggle, though, he demonstrates that he understands God had not thoroughly abandoned him. Yet he pleads: *how long will you hide your face from me?* He is yearning for the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6: *The Lord bless you and keep you; the Lord make His face shine upon you and be gracious to you; the Lord lift up His countenance upon you and give you peace.* David longs for the face of God — for in his crying he's asking: God, let your face shine upon me, be gracious to me, and give me peace. David has no peace.

He feels overwhelmed and crushed, and in his despair he asks: *God, I take counsel in my soul and have sorrow in my heart all the day.* David's initial reaction had been to turn inward and to seek solace in his emotions — yet he is reaping only sorrow. David is allowing his feelings and emotions to dictate his understanding and perception of the situation.

Counsel is advice — it is an endeavor of the mind in which one relates facts to another. Given these facts, this is how we should proceed. But what David has acknowledged is that in his despair he is not gaining counsel from what he knows in his mind, but from what he thinks he knows in his heart — in his soul — the seat of his emotions. He is allowing his emotions to drive his mind and his actions. And in his tears he is asking: *how long must this sorrow control me?*

Have you ever felt that before? Can you relate to David's struggle? I certainly can.

While deployed to Afghanistan, there was a point where my body and mind were exhausted. I was discouraged, depressed, I felt alone. I emailed Bethany that I was feeling low. It was already late in the day, and since I had not heard back from her, I went to bed for much-needed rest. When I awoke in the morning, I pulled out my phone and saw she had responded. I was about to read her words of confidence and encouragement. I opened it and it said: *Drink water.*

I burst out laughing. At the time she was unsure if I meant that I was feeling physically low — getting sick — or mentally low. Yet in the light of the morning, I had my perspective changed, and I could laugh at this strange advice.

Believe this: Christ is the light of your morning. Isaiah 30:18: *O Lord, be gracious to us; we wait for You. Be our arm every morning, our salvation in the time of trouble.* And Jeremiah likewise professed in Lamentations 3: *The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases; His mercies never come to an end. They are new every morning. Great is Your faithfulness. The Lord is my portion, says my soul, therefore I will hope in Him.*

David does not identify what his specific struggle is or what led to his depression. In verse 2 he asks: *how long will my enemy be exalted over me?* He references a singular enemy — but he does not tell us who that enemy is. I believe this is intentional for the sacred text. David has a specific adversary in mind, but I argue that does not matter for the modern believer. It doesn't matter whether your struggle is the same as David's struggle. A Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair.

You will meet parents who have wept for years over their wayward children, crying out: *how long, O Lord?* You will meet others dealing with chronic illnesses offering no reprieve. Others with recurring forms of deep depression. Those who have lost loved ones — parents, children, spouses, miscarriages — all crying out: *how long, O Lord?*

When you find yourself there, you need to ask: is Jesus enough? And know for certain that yes, a Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair.

## Point 2: The Means of Grace Tether the Weary to Christ

How do we find our peace in Christ when we are in the depths of depression? Friends, there is no other way than the ordinary means of grace: prayer, the preaching of the Word, and the sacraments.

The means of grace are the ordinary means in which every believer — especially the weary — is tethered to Christ. In my mind I can picture a balloon on a string, a flag on a flagpole, or even a tarp with just a single strap. You might feel like you're out there flapping in your misery — but that single strap, that anchor chain, is the means of grace securing you to Christ.

In verses 1-2 David pours out his heart to God in prayer. He is doing the right thing. He knows he is sick, and he's going to the doctor. David knew he was wrestling in his depression and he cried out to the one who hears and answers. In Psalm 17 he wrote: *I have called upon You, for You will hear me, O God; incline Your ear to me, and hear my speech.*

Dr. Kurtow of Greenville Presbyterian Theological Seminary has been quoted often: "If you want to get hit by a train, you play on the railroad tracks. If you want to find grace, you go to where the means of grace can be found."

A Christian in despair can find peace — but he needs to approach the throne of grace for it. He needs to be in church to hear the Word of God preached. He needs to participate in the sacraments and cling to his baptism. He needs to be reading his Bible. He needs to come to God in prayer. Granted, in the last few months we have been restricted in our ability for public worship. But even in quarantine, we have a clear path to the throne of grace through reading of the Bible, private worship, and prayer.

We heard David's mourning in verses 1-2. Now in verse 3 we hear his supplication: *Consider and hear me, O Lord my God. Lighten my eyes.*

This is personal. This is relational. David is crying out to Yahweh, the covenantal name of God. In essence, David is saying: God, we have an agreement. We have a covenant — a relationship. You said: *I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God, and you shall know that I am the Lord your God.* So David prays: *Consider and hear me, O Yahweh, my God.*

As David's prayer continues, we see a change in tone — a transition from weeping to supplication. He no longer asks: *how long?* But simply: *give light to my eyes.*

Scripture illustrates that God possesses literal brilliant light. Daniel 2:22 declares: *He knows what is in the darkness, and light dwells with him.* Nothing is hidden from his presence. There is no such thing as a dark corner in God's world.

In Psalm 36:9 the Psalmist declares: *For with You is the fountain of life; in Your light we see light.* Ultimately our redemption is found in the light of Christ. Jesus declared: *I am the light of the world. He who follows Me shall not walk in darkness, but have the light of life.*

David prayed because he knew it worked. In Psalm 18: *I will call upon the Lord, who is worthy to be praised; so shall I be saved from my enemies. The pangs of death surrounded me and the floods of ungodliness made me afraid. The sorrows of Sheol surrounded me; the snares of death confronted me. In my distress I called upon the Lord, and cried out to my God; He heard my voice from His temple, and my cry came before Him, even to His ears.*

David needed the light of God's wisdom to change his perspective. And he needed the light of his Savior to rescue him from his enemies. David needs light to his eyes — he says: *lest I sleep the sleep of death. Lest my enemies say, "I have prevailed over him." Lest my foes rejoice when I am shaken.*

His concern is that his despair will drive him to death. We echo the words of Paul: *For me to live is Christ, and to die is gain* — yet we do not desire that our death be in any way a victory for the enemy. Give me light, lest I die. Give me light, lest my enemies triumph and cause me to lose heart, to give up, to die.

From Psalm 119:77: *Let Your tender mercies come to me that I may live.* David often faced literal death by enemies — which is less common for the average Western Christian. But the crushing blows of depression and other trials, the grief of significant loss, bankruptcy, feelings of incompetence, a marriage without affection, a boss who pushes you down, detractors who hate you or other spiritual warfare — these are no less important. The remedy is the same: we fall on the wisdom of our Savior to be light to our eyes.

## Point 3: Peace Returns in the Light of Jesus

David demonstrated — and I can attest — that we can face the threat of physical death with confidence. We can face our depression, our anxiety, our illness, by having a proper perspective on our life in Christ. Peace returns in the light of Jesus.

In Psalm 30, David was moved to praise: *I will extol You, for You have lifted me up, and have not let my foes rejoice over me. O Lord my God, I cried out to You, and You healed me... You have turned for me my mourning into dancing; You have put off my sackcloth and clothed me with gladness... For His anger is but for a moment, His favor is for life; weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning.*

At the threat of physical death, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah declared in Daniel 3: *O Nebuchadnezzar, we have no need to answer you in this matter. If that is the case, our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace, and He will deliver us from your hand, O king. But if not, let it be known to you, O king, that we do not serve your gods, nor will we worship the gold image which you have set up.*

Our God is able to deliver us. He will deliver us. And even if he doesn't deliver us right now in this moment, we serve and worship him alone. And as we know, in their trial, Christ met with them personally and entered into the furnace with them and ministered to them until they were brought out alive. What a beautiful picture.

What these men were also testifying was that even in death, God was their Redeemer and Savior. They were familiar with the words of Job in Job 19:25-27: *For I know that my Redeemer lives, and He shall stand at last on the earth. And after my skin is destroyed, this I know, that in my flesh I shall see God, whom I shall see for myself, and my eyes shall behold, and not another. How my heart yearns within me!*

Psalm 13 demonstrates through David's progression that a Christian can find peace even in the deepest despair. He concludes:

*But I have trusted in Your mercy; my heart shall rejoice in Your salvation. I will sing to the Lord, because He has dealt bountifully with me.*

David exhibits trust in the covenantal lovingkindness of God — promised to his covenant children. In verse 5, what the New King James translates as "mercy" is the Hebrew hesed. This is my favorite Hebrew word. It's referring to an enduring faithfulness, a steadfast love, a promise-keeping and covenantal love that will not fail. It is a weighty word packed with rich meaning and significant undertones of deep relational love.

In Psalm 18:19, David states: *He delivered me because He delighted in me.* Our hearts can rejoice in our salvation, in our reconciliation with God. Christ saved us from his wrath and judgment and the sting of death. Believer, you are held now in a covenantal bond with your Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. And he declares: *I will never leave you nor forsake you.*

David had faith in his Redeemer — even though he had not yet seen him — and this led him to sing praise to his covenantal God. We have the joy of looking back to Christ who paid for our reconciliation. That should absolutely lead us to praise.

For David to get to that place where he could clear the lump from his throat and dry his eyes, he had to experience a change of perspective. He used the means of grace to cry out to God in prayer, and God gave him the light of Christ to change his perspective. David's peace returned in the light of his Savior — and the same can be true for you, believer. Peace returns in the light of Jesus.

I do have a tendency to go back and forth in Scripture for illustrations, and I'm going to leave you with one more. In John 20, we find Mary in the garden three days after the death of Jesus Christ. She's weeping. She's discouraged. She's confused. She hears a voice behind her, asking: *Why are you weeping? For whom are you seeking?* Mary was in the garden looking for Jesus — she thought his body had been moved. She thought she was speaking to the gardener. She probably couldn't see through the tears in her eyes.

And what was Jesus' response? *Mary.* With simply the sound of her name being called by her Savior, her perspective changed in a moment, and she cried out: *Teacher!* He was not dead. He had not been moved. He was alive and well. He was there for her. And he called her name.

Believer, the same is true for us today. Our Lord is risen. He is here. He is with us. Your Lord and Savior is with you — and peace returns in the light of Christ.

This morning I ask you: is Jesus enough? Believer, you need to know that indeed Jesus is enough. He has to be. Peter answered Jesus this way: *Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life. And we have believed and have come to know that You are the Holy One of God.*

A Christian can find peace even in his despair. Even Christians face deep despair. Yet the means of grace tether the weary to Christ. And through the means of grace, peace returns in the light of Jesus. Is Jesus enough? Yes.

If you are not already in the habit, begin a pattern today of private Scripture reading and prayer — so that when you are hit with the adversity of life and your enemies are seeking your death, you can turn to your Lord and Savior and declare: Jesus is enough. I have peace even in my despair.

Someone may be here today who cannot relate to the peace in Christ of which I've preached. It is your sin that separates you from the love of God. Yet the good news of the gospel is that Jesus came — in his life, his death, and his resurrection — to pay the price that justice demands. Humble yourself today. Cry out to God for the forgiveness of your sins and cling to Christ in faith. *Behold, now is the favorable time. Behold, now is the day of salvation.* If you have questions or concerns, please feel free to come and speak to me after the service or to find one of the elders.

Our Heavenly Father, we do ask that Your face would shine upon us, that You would give us peace, that You would give us wisdom to face the adversities and trials of life. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
